guest_role: Co-founder and General Partner at Better Tomorrow Ventures (fintech-focused pre-seed/seed fund)
assertiveness_why: Harry repeatedly plants explicit counter-theses (on VC collaboration, LATAM funding depth, emerging-market retrenchment, GP commit size) and directly says 'I disagree' multiple times, even narrating his own evolution as an interviewer from agreeable to argumentative.
question_style: Conversational but frequently adversarial: Harry states his own view first (e.g. venture has become 'sharp elbowed', LATAM lacks growth-stage capital) and challenges the guest to rebut it, rather than just asking open questions; he also self-referentially notes he now says 'I disagree' where he used to just agree.
tone: Very matey and irreverent, laced with profanity and personal banter (pizza toppings, chocolate in the fridge, dating jokes), high energy, clearly a fan but not deferential.
depth: Digs into fund mechanics with real numbers — entry valuations, ownership percentages, reserve ratios, capital efficiency vs headline valuation, GP commit sizing — and pushes on specific portfolio company pricing and follow-on decisions.
harry_quotes: I disagree on Latin. I don't know if we have the lifecycle funding in Latin that we think we do.
Do you agree with me and how do you think about where we are in the sliding scale of collaboration?
I am worried. I am worried, Sheil.
learnings: Capital-efficient exits can return more fund value than larger but heavily-diluted outcomes, since ownership percentage matters more than headline valuation.
GP commits that represent too large a share of a manager's net worth can distort fund decisions (e.g. pushing for premature secondary sales).
A model failing with one founder doesn't mean the model is broken — execution quality can make the same idea work the second time (chime vs. simple).

Emerging markets are diverging: India and Latin America now have enough local fund infrastructure to support companies through multiple stages, while Africa and Pakistan are more exposed to capital retrenchment.
Fund managers should stay disciplined on entry price but go all-in on supporting the founder once invested, regardless of later round pricing.
notable: Harry explicitly narrates his own growth as an interviewer mid-episode, contrasting his past tendency to agree with his current willingness to openly disagree with guests.
